title: The Research Workspace: Asking 'What If' Safely
learningObjectives: Design an experiment structure for testing a specific hypothesis against the Digital Twin (e.g. 'would adding 20% more solar capacity reduce cost by more than it would destabilize the grid')
Run a controlled comparison between a baseline scenario and a modified scenario using the integrated system
Report an experiment's result with the same rigor established throughout the curriculum: a specific claim, the evidence for it, and its limitations
Distinguish a research question the Digital Twin can actually answer from one that would require data or capabilities the system doesn't have
whyItMatters: The dashboard answers 'what should I do right now.' A grid operator, researcher, or investor also needs to ask 'what if we changed something structural' — more solar capacity, a different battery size, a new substation — and testing that safely, in simulation, before touching anything real, is exactly the original mission's stated advantage: you don't need permission from a utility to start proving value in simulation.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's Research Workspace — a structured environment for testing hypotheses about grid changes safely in simulation, delivering on the original mission's explicit strategy of proving value in simulation before approaching real infrastructure.

problem.summary: Nothing yet lets you ask 'what if the grid were different' rigorously.
problem.content: Every capability built so far answers operational questions about the current grid as it exists. Nobody has built a way to ask 'what if the grid were different' — more solar, a bigger battery, an additional substation — and get a rigorous, comparable answer.

motivation.summary: Simulated evidence is the exact strategic advantage the mission was scoped around.
motivation.content: This is precisely the strategic advantage the mission's original scoping conversation identified: you don't need utility company permission to test hypotheses in your own simulator, and once you can demonstrate a consistent, well-evidenced improvement in simulation, that becomes real evidence to bring to an actual conversation with a utility, researcher, or investor.

theory.summary: A rigorous experiment needs a hypothesis, a controlled baseline/modified comparison, and honest scope limits.
theory.content: A rigorous simulated experiment needs a clearly stated hypothesis, a baseline scenario (the current grid configuration, run through the full integrated system), a modified scenario (the same system with one specific structural change), and a controlled comparison holding everything else constant — reusing the exact same evaluation discipline from Mission 3's baseline-and-leakage lesson and Mission 4's honest-verdict lesson, now applied to structural 'what if' questions rather than model comparisons. A well-scoped research question is one the Digital Twin's actual data and models can address — 'would 20% more solar reduce cost' is testable with what you've built; 'would this policy change consumer behavior' is not, because nothing in the Digital Twin models consumer behavioral response, and a rigorous research workspace should say so explicitly rather than attempting an answer beyond its actual scope.

visualExplanation.summary: Baseline and modified scenarios run through the identical pipeline; out-of-scope questions get flagged.
visualExplanation.content: A baseline scenario and a modified scenario run through the identical integrated-system pipeline side by side, with their outputs (cost, emissions, resilience) compared in a results panel, and an 'out of scope' flag shown for a question the twin's data can't actually address.

interactiveDemo.summary: Define a structural change, run both scenarios, and see an out-of-scope question flagged.
interactiveDemo.content: Define a structural change (adjust solar capacity, battery size) on a live twin, run both baseline and modified scenarios, and inspect a generated comparison report, then attempt a question the twin can't actually answer and observe the workspace correctly flag it as out of scope.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: run a controlled baseline-vs-modified experiment.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
def run_experiment(hypothesis, baseline_config, modified_config, ai_energy_os):
    baseline_results = ai_energy_os.simulate(baseline_config)
    modified_results = ai_energy_os.simulate(modified_config)
    return {
        "hypothesis": hypothesis,
        "cost_change": modified_results["total_cost"] - baseline_results["total_cost"],
        "emissions_change": modified_results["total_emissions"] - baseline_results["total_emissions"],
        "resilience_change": modified_results["min_cut_capacity"] - baseline_results["min_cut_capacity"],
    }
```

independentCoding.summary: Design and run a real battery-capacity resilience experiment.
independentCoding.content: Design and run an experiment testing whether increasing battery capacity at your grid's most capacity-constrained substation (identified back in Mission 5) measurably improves resilience without significantly increasing cost.

debugging.summary: Fix a confounded comparison using different underlying demand data for each scenario.
debugging.content: An experiment comparison runs the baseline and modified scenarios against different underlying demand data (one used raw historical data, the other used a resampled version), invalidating the comparison despite both individually looking correct. Identify the confound and fix the experiment to hold everything but the tested variable constant.

reflection.summary: A confounded comparison is as useless as no experiment at all.
reflection.content: Why does a confounded comparison — where more than one thing differs between baseline and modified scenarios — produce a result that's just as useless as no experiment at all, even if every individual number is computed correctly?

teachBack.summary: Explain the difference between an answerable and an unanswerable research question.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the difference between a research question your Digital Twin can rigorously answer and one it can't, using a concrete example of each from your own experimentation.

miniProject.summary: Design, run, and report a real structural experiment with stated scope limits.
miniProject.content: Design, run, and report on a real structural 'what if' experiment using your integrated AI Energy OS, following the full baseline/modified/controlled-comparison discipline, with an explicit statement of what the result does and doesn't prove.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This realizes the mission's original insight: proving value in simulation before approaching real infrastructure.
portfolioEvidence.content: This research workspace is the concrete realization of the mission's original strategic insight: proving value in simulation, safely, before ever needing to approach real infrastructure with anything more than an idea.

labs.title: Controlled Experiment
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise designing and running a controlled baseline-vs-modified experiment against the integrated system, requiring a fix for a planted confounded-comparison bug.

simulations.title: Baseline vs. Modified Scenario Visualizer
simulations.description: Side-by-side simulation of a baseline and a structurally modified grid scenario, comparing cost, emissions, and resilience outputs.

flashcards.front: Why is 'more than one thing differs between baseline and modified scenarios' a fatal flaw in an experiment, not a minor issue?
flashcards.back: It confounds the comparison — you can no longer attribute any observed difference specifically to the variable you intended to test, since something else also changed and could be the real cause.

flashcards.front: Why should a research workspace explicitly flag an out-of-scope question rather than attempt an answer?
flashcards.back: Attempting an answer beyond what the twin's actual data and models can support would produce a result that looks like evidence but isn't grounded in anything real — explicitly flagging the limitation is more honest and more useful than a fabricated answer.

rubric: Experiment holds every variable but the tested one constant between baseline and modified scenarios
Correctly diagnoses and fixes the confounded-comparison bug from mismatched underlying data
Final report explicitly states what the result does and doesn't prove, including any out-of-scope aspects
